bio.photo_notes: Beebe Hooker Strong (1866-1933), prominent Baraboo Wisconsin businessman and local historian

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee per FA

career.organization: Baraboo Gas & Electric Light Company, Baraboo, Wisconsin
career.notes: W.H. Hopkins was president; B.H. Strong treasurer per period records.
Compiled and preserved historical accounts of the Baraboo region; documented account of how the Baraboo River received its name. Also managed local real estate.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the maternal Hooker ancestry — his mother was Rosa Searls Warner Strong, wife of Robert Hunter Strong, whose mother Dolly Goodwin Hooker carried the Hooker surname directly.
First name 'Beebe' honors the Beebe family — his great-grandfather Judge Elisha BEEBE Strong (I00900) took the Beebe surname from his mother Mary Beebe Strong of Windsor, CT.

cross_connections.display_label: Both bore the Hooker name through the Strong family, 90 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: The name 'Hooker' traveled from Farmington to Milwaukee
narrative_blocks.body: Beebe Hooker Strong was born in Milwaukee in June 1866, bearing a middle name that traces a 137-year arc: his great-great-grandmother Sarah Hooker of Farmington, Connecticut had married into the Strong family in 1729, her great-great-grandson Asahel Hooker Strong preserved the name in Connecticut in 1776, and his grandmother Dolly Goodwin Hooker married a Strong again in 1813. The 'Hooker' in his name was a deliberate act of genealogical memory — a Wisconsin family's way of honoring the Hartford Puritan founder in whose blood they knew themselves to share.

narrative_blocks.header: Preserved how the Baraboo River got its name
narrative_blocks.body: Beebe Hooker Strong became a prominent civic figure in Baraboo, Wisconsin — Treasurer of the Baraboo Gas & Electric Light Company — where he compiled and preserved the documented account explaining how the Baraboo River received its unusual name, a piece of regional oral history that would otherwise have passed unrecorded. The same instinct that drove him to document local origins animated his family's naming practice: they called him 'Hooker' so that no descendant would forget where they came from, and he spent his career ensuring Baraboo didn't forget where it came from either.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
History of Descendants of Elder John Strong, Vol. 1 (1871)

Wisconsin Historical Society — Newspaper Records BA5205

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee — same ground as her father Beebe Hooker Strong

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-nine; he became Wisconsin's radical voice
narrative_blocks.body: Marian Caldwell Strong Amlie died on May 4, 1930 at age twenty-nine — just one year before her husband Thomas Ryum Amlie was first elected to Congress, and two years before he became one of the most radical legislative voices of the New Deal era, described by the Wall Street Journal as sponsoring legislation to put 'the federal government in control of all industry by practical socialization.' She was buried at Forest Home Cemetery in Milwaukee, the same ground that holds her father Beebe Hooker Strong — both a Wisconsin civic leader and his daughter, both returned to the cemetery Milwaukee's pioneers had established.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
History of Descendants of Elder John Strong, Vol. 1 (1871)

bio.photo_notes: Thomas Ryum Amlie (1897-1973), US Congressman from Wisconsin

burial.plot_notes: Cremated; remains interred Forest Hill Cemetery Madison (cenotaph) per FA

notable.notable_blurb: US Congressman from Wisconsin; Progressive, Republican, and Democrat

education.notes: Also attended Univ of North Dakota 1916-18; Univ of Minnesota 1 year.

career.organization: Fiedler, Garrigan, and Amlie; Beloit, then Elkhorn, Wisconsin

career.notes: Elected as Republican; filled vacancy from death of Henry Allen Cooper.
Elected on Wisconsin Progressive Party ticket.

career.organization: Union for Democratic Action (later Americans for Democratic Action)

career.notes: Withdrew from practice for health reasons 1962.

cross_connections.display_label: Both US Congressmen who married into the Hooker-Strong family

narrative_blocks.header: FDR wrote him: 'You are still young'
narrative_blocks.body: When conservative forces in Congress moved against Thomas Amlie's nomination to the Interstate Commerce Commission in 1939 — the Wisconsin Assembly resolved that he 'advocated scrapping of our Federal Constitution' — President Roosevelt wrote him personally upon his withdrawal: 'A quarter of a century ago I, too, was called a Communist and a wild-eyed radical because I fought for factory inspection, for a fifty-four-hour-a-week bill for women and children in industry. You are still young and I hope that you will continue to work for the improvement of social and economic legislation.' The exchange is one of the most revealing documents of the New Deal's inner ideological life.

narrative_blocks.header: Voted on Spain; Hans enlisted two days after
narrative_blocks.body: In 1937, Amlie voted with the overwhelming majority of Congress to ban the export of arms to either side in the Spanish Civil War — and just two days after the vote, his brother Hans Amlie was reported to have volunteered for the International Brigades fighting on behalf of the Spanish Republic. Hans survived to become a commander of the Lincoln Battalion; Thomas later supported a fundraising drive to bring wounded American volunteers home from Spain, a rare instance of a sitting congressman openly aligning with the cause his own sibling had bled for.

narrative_blocks.header: One of history's most radical elected Republicans
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Amlie's DW-Nominate score of -0.322 is one of the lowest ever recorded for anyone who served in Congress as a Republican — second perhaps only to East Harlem's Vito Marcantonio. He believed openly that capitalism was dying and said so publicly at the League for Independent Political Action convention in 1933; in Congress he sponsored legislation the Wall Street Journal described as designed to put 'the federal government in control of all industry by practical socialization,' yet he won his seat representing a Wisconsin farm district that had been electing progressives since the La Follette era.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped found what became Americans for Democratic Action
narrative_blocks.body: In 1941, Amlie became a founding member and director of the Union for Democratic Action — a liberal organization that, distinguishing itself from fellow-traveler groups, explicitly barred Communists from joining — and pioneered the practice of grading members of Congress on their voting records to produce public liberal scorecards. That organization became Americans for Democratic Action in 1946, the most influential postwar liberal political organization in the United States; the methodological work Amlie began in tracking congressional votes became the template by which the ADA held Congress accountable for a generation.

quotes.text: A quarter of a century ago I, too, was called a Communist and a wild-eyed radical because I fought for factory inspection, for a fifty-four-hour-a-week bill for women and children in industry and similar measures. You are still young and I hope that you will continue to work for the improvement of social and economic legislation under our framework of Government.
quotes.attribution: Franklin D. Roosevelt, letter to Thomas Amlie on withdrawal of ICC nomination, April 17, 1939

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee WI per FA

career.organization: Kellogg & Strong Steamboat Company and Warehouse, Milwaukee

narrative_blocks.header: Helped secure the land for Forest Home Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was instrumental in securing the land for Forest Home Cemetery in Milwaukee and persuading St. Paul's Episcopal Church to establish the cemetery there — a civic contribution that anchored Milwaukee's most prominent burial ground. He came to Wisconsin around 1830, clerked in the territorial legislature in 1839-40, built his fortune through the Kellogg & Strong Steamboat company and Newberry Furnace, and was twice married.

narrative_blocks.header: Bridged two Strong-Hooker marriages and two frontier states
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was the son of the second Strong-Hooker marriage — Judge Elisha Beebe Strong and Dolly Goodwin Hooker — and the father of the man who would carry that name to a third generation: Beebe Hooker Strong, born Milwaukee 1866. He came to Wisconsin around 1830 at age ten, making the journey from the Rochester land grants his father had built to the Great Lakes frontier, and with him the Strong-Hooker legacy crossed into a new American West.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
History of Descendants of Elder John Strong, Vol. 1 (1871)

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her Milwaukee pioneer husband by nineteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Rosa Searls Warner Strong, widowed when Robert Hunter Strong died at Waukesha in May 1892, lived on for nineteen more years — dying in Chicago on July 14, 1911 at age 87 while visiting her daughter, having outlasted her husband long enough to see her children established across three states. Her obituary noted that Robert had been instrumental in founding Forest Home Cemetery and that the family had been prominent at St. Paul's Episcopal Church — the church whose cemetery he helped build would ultimately receive them both.

sources.label: FindAGrave

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

notable.notable_blurb: First Judge of Monroe County, NY; Yale 1809

education.notes: Prepared for college by Nathan Johnson. Yale class of 1809.
Attended Litchfield Law School 1810; thereafter read law in Canandaigua NY.

career.notes: Partner with William H. Adams. Admitted to bar 1812.
Purchased 1,000 acres near Rochester with Elisha Beach; developed Carthage area; erected storehouses, incline railway, bridge over lower falls.
Appointed March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton. Monroe County organized February 23, 1821.
Moved to Detroit 1861; died there Oct 14, 1867.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1810; appointed first judge of Monroe County NY by 1821.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Strong men who married Hooker descendants, 84 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: First Judge of Monroe County, appointed 1821
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Beebe Strong was appointed the first judge of Monroe County, New York on March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton — just days after the county itself was organized by the state legislature from Ontario County territory. A Yale graduate (1809) and Litchfield Law School alumnus, he had also served in the New York State Assembly in 1819-1820 and as a presidential elector in 1824, becoming one of the most prominent legal figures in upstate New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage repeated a Strong family tradition
narrative_blocks.body: When Elisha Beebe Strong married Dolly Goodwin Hooker in 1813 at Canandaigua, New York, he was not the first Strong to wed a Thomas Hooker descendant: 84 years earlier, his ancestral kin Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married Sarah Hooker of Farmington, Connecticut in 1729, producing a line that named a grandson 'Asahel Hooker Strong' to preserve the memory. The two unions — Farmington 1729, Canandaigua 1813 — trace the same Strong-Hooker affinity across the Great Migration and the early Republic, from a Farmington lawyer's household to a frontier judge's courtroom on the Erie Canal.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband was Monroe County's first judge
narrative_blocks.body: Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong, who in 1821 became the first judge of Monroe County, New York. She died in Rochester in 1850; he remarried and moved to Detroit, dying there in 1867.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Hooker-Strong marriage echoed one from 1729
narrative_blocks.body: When Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813, she entered a family that had already wed into the Hooker line 84 years before: Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married her distant cousin Sarah Hooker at Farmington in 1729, and a grandson of that marriage — Asahel Hooker Strong — was already carrying her maiden name as his own middle name by 1776. Dolly's marriage completed the second arc; her great-grandson Beebe Hooker Strong, born in Milwaukee in 1866, would carry the name to a third generation on the Wisconsin frontier.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

burialCemetery.notes: Robert Hunter Strong helped secure the land and establish this cemetery through St. Paul's Episcopal Church.
